role_summary: Folk singer and civil rights movement songbook contributor; helped popularize "We Shall Overcome" as a movement anthem from Highlander Folk School onward; co-founder of the Almanac Singers (1941) and the Weavers (1950); songwriter of "Where Have All the Flowers Gone?", "If I Had a Hammer", and "Turn! Turn! Turn!".

ai_reading: Seeger's top semantic neighbor is the joint Candie and Guy Carawan interview (entry 11, count 73), the Highlander Folk School music teachers; this is the same anchor that Jamila Jones (entry 58, here at count 66, Seeger's second neighbor) clusters around. The embedding therefore reads Seeger as part of the Highlander-music-tradition vector cluster: he, the Carawans, and Jamila Jones form a triadic signature in the corpus, the freedom-song-pedagogy node that the catalog separately treats in those interviewees' pages. Seeger's other neighbors are Charles McLaurin (entry 17, Mississippi SNCC), Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29, SCLC Educational Director), and C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC). The novel observation is on the Nonviolence as Theology vs Armed Self-Defense axis where Seeger sits at +0.50, well toward Armed Self-Defense. For a folksinger whose civil-rights-era songbook is itself the nonviolent movement's musical canon, the cultural reading would predict a strong Nonviolence-as-Theology placement; the embedding pulls his testimony toward the opposite pole, consistent with the Wyatt Tee Walker pattern (entry 132) of the catalog's SCLC-and-folkmusic figures inverting on this axis.
biographical_paragraph: Seeger helped popularize "We Shall Overcome" as a civil rights movement anthem from the Highlander Folk School onward; he organized a 1963 Carnegie Hall benefit concert for Highlander that featured the Freedom Singers, and he sang the anthem alongside roughly a thousand other participants on the 1965 Selma to Montgomery marches [src: 2]. Born May 3, 1919, he co-founded the Almanac Singers in 1941 and the reconstituted Weavers in 1950; the Weavers' recording of Lead Belly's "Goodnight, Irene" topped the charts for thirteen weeks that year [src: 2]. His own compositions "Where Have All the Flowers Gone?", "If I Had a Hammer", and "Turn! Turn! Turn!" entered the folk-revival canon of the period [src: 2]. He died January 27, 2014, at ninety-four [src: 2]. His memoir Where Have All the Flowers Gone was first published in 1993, reissued in 2009 [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 97 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: Seeger's 1948 arrangement of "We Shall Overcome" is the pivotal pedagogical link between the labor-movement hymn the song descended from and the freedom-song corpus the southern movement carried across the 1960s; the version he developed (a fingerstyle banjo accompaniment paired with the chord progression that became standard) was the version Zilphia Horton had collected at Highlander Folk School in 1947 from striking Charleston tobacco workers, the version Seeger taught to the Highlander music workshops in the late 1940s and 1950s, and the version Guy Carawan in turn would teach to the SNCC founding cohort at Raleigh in April 1960 (see the Candie and Guy Carawan joint interview at catalog entry 11 for the Highlander music-pedagogy line of transmission) [src: 2]. The August 19, 1955 Seeger appearance before the House Un-American Activities Committee was the procedural turning point in his Cold War political prosecution: Seeger refused to invoke the Fifth Amendment and instead asserted First Amendment grounds in declining to answer questions about his political associations and the political content of his songs, a position the committee treated as contempt of Congress [src: 3]. The Justice Department indicted Seeger on ten counts in 1957, and a federal jury convicted him in March 1961; Judge Thomas F. Murphy sentenced him to one year in prison the following month [src: 3]. The United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit overturned the conviction on May 18, 1962, on the technical ground that the indictment had failed to plead the contempt counts adequately, ending the prosecution without reaching the First Amendment question Seeger had raised [src: 3]. The Weavers, the quartet Seeger had co-founded with Lee Hays, Ronnie Gilbert, and Fred Hellerman in November 1948, ran a brief commercial peak from 1949 through 1952, anchored by the thirteen-week chart run of their 1950 Decca recording of Lead Belly's "Goodnight, Irene" [src: 4]. The June 1950 publication of the anti-communist pamphlet Red Channels named Seeger and Hays among 151 entertainment-industry figures alleged to have communist sympathies; the resulting broadcast and concert blacklist collapsed the Weavers' commercial bookings by 1952, and the group disbanded the same year [src: 4]. The 1969 founding of the Hudson River Sloop Clearwater was Seeger's late-career organizational commitment: he led the construction of the 106-foot sloop Clearwater at the Harvey Gamage shipyard in South Bristol, Maine, launched June 27, 1969, and organized the vessel's deployment as a Hudson River environmental-education program targeting industrial pollution of the river through public sailing programs and waterfront festivals [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: Seeger received the 1993 Kennedy Center Honors alongside Marion Williams, Johnny Carson, Arthur Mitchell, and Georg Solti, the federal cultural honor presented annually at the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts in recognition of lifetime contribution to the performing arts [src: 6]. The January 18, 2009 We Are One inaugural concert at the Lincoln Memorial, two days before the swearing-in of President Barack Obama, featured Seeger and Bruce Springsteen leading the audience in a performance of Woody Guthrie's "This Land Is Your Land" that included the verses Guthrie had written but that subsequent recordings had often omitted (the verses naming private-property fences and relief-office lines as part of the song's depiction of the American landscape) [src: 7]. Seeger died at New York-Presbyterian Hospital in Manhattan on January 27, 2014, at age ninety-four; he had been admitted six days earlier following a fall [src: 2]. The Pete Seeger Papers are held by the American Folklife Center at the Library of Congress (collection AFC 2018/004), comprising correspondence, manuscripts, photographs, sound recordings, and ephemera that document Seeger's musical career and his political and environmental organizing across the twentieth and early twenty-first centuries [src: 8]. The 2007 memoir Pete Seeger in His Own Words, edited by Rob Rosenthal and Sam Rosenthal, and the longer collaborative work Pete Seeger: In His Own Words assembled with David Bernz across the same period, are the principal first-person published sources for the Seeger archive that the LoC holdings catalog [src: 9].

gallery.alt: Pete Seeger arriving at federal court with his guitar, April 4, 1961
gallery.caption: Pete Seeger arriving at federal court in New York with his guitar case, April 4, 1961, during the contempt of Congress proceedings that followed his August 19, 1955 appearance before the House Un-American Activities Committee. A federal jury convicted Seeger in March 1961 on ten counts; Judge Thomas F. Murphy sentenced him to a year in prison the following month. The United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit overturned the conviction on May 18, 1962. Library of Congress New York World-Telegram and Sun Newspaper Photograph Collection.
gallery.photographer: Walter Albertin (New York World-Telegram and Sun staff photographer)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons; original from the Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division (LC-USZ62-130860)
gallery.license: Public domain (no known copyright restrictions; Library of Congress NYWTS collection)

gallery.alt: Pete Seeger performing at the Clearwater Festival, June 16, 2007
gallery.caption: Seeger performing at the Clearwater Festival at age 88, June 16, 2007. The festival is the principal annual fundraising and public-programming event for the Hudson River Sloop Clearwater, the environmental-education organization Seeger founded in 1969 around the 106-foot sloop launched that June at the Harvey Gamage shipyard in South Bristol, Maine. The 2007 festival photograph postdates Seeger's late-career consolidation of the Clearwater program as the institutional vehicle for his Hudson River-pollution organizing.

gallery.alt: Pete Seeger at the Newport Folk Festival, August 2009
gallery.caption: Seeger at the Newport Folk Festival, August 1, 2009, eight months after the January 18, 2009 We Are One inaugural concert at the Lincoln Memorial where he and Bruce Springsteen had led the audience in a performance of Woody Guthrie's "This Land Is Your Land" that restored the song's full Guthrie verses. The Newport Folk Festival, which Seeger had helped programmatically anchor across the 1960s folk-revival decade, remained one of his principal late-career performing venues across the 2000s.
